failure_surface: Test case evaluation returned 0, indicating the output file did not pass verification despite successful code execution.
root_cause: Using pandas.to_excel() to write the output caused loss of original workbook formatting, formulas, and cell styles. The task required preserving the .xlsx file structure while selectively deleting columns, but the agent chose a workflow that converts all content to static values during load-write cycle.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: When editing an existing .xlsx file, load and modify using openpyxl Workbook objects directly instead of converting to pandas DataFrames for operations that must preserve formatting and formulas.
evidence: Code used pd.read_excel() which loads data as DataFrame, converting formulas to static values
Output saved via df_filtered.to_excel() which does not retain fonts, colors, borders, or number formats per SKILL.md Section 5
Script executed successfully (logs show 415 columns removed), yet test_case_results shows [0]
SKILL.md Section 2 Warning states: 'pandas.read_excel() converts formulas to static values. Use openpyxl if formulas must be retained'

memory_items.title: Preserve Formatting When Modifying Existing Files
memory_items.lesson: When the task involves editing an existing .xlsx file, use openpyxl to load, modify, and save to preserve formatting, cell styles, and merged cells.
memory_items.generalization: pandas.to_excel() loses all styling information; openpyxl Workbook objects maintain them through read-modify-write cycles.

memory_items.title: Formulas Are Static Values in Pandas
memory_items.lesson: Any formula present in the original sheet will be converted to its calculated value when loaded via pandas; use openpyxl cell.value access to preserve formula strings.
memory_items.generalization: Never rely on pandas if downstream calculations depend on existing formulas being intact.

memory_items.title: Select Tool Based on What Must Be Preserved
memory_items.lesson: Evaluate whether the task requires preserving formatting/formulas before choosing between pandas (data analysis) and openpyxl (structural/styling preservation).
memory_items.generalization: For pure data transformations where style doesn't matter, pandas is acceptable. For file editing where structure/style/formulas matter, openpyxl is mandatory.

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Formatting Preservation During Column Deletion
When deleting columns from an existing `.xlsx` file, do not rely on the pandas read-modify-write cycle (`read_excel()` + `to_excel()`). This workflow converts all cell content to static values and discards all visual formatting. Instead, load the workbook with `openpyxl`, perform column deletions directly on the worksheet object, then save the workbook. This preserves cell styles, formulas, number formats, and any conditional formatting rules.

### Formula Integrity Check Before Choosing Tools
Before implementing column/row manipulation logic, check if the source file contains formulas (use `openpyxl` to inspect cell.data_type or cell.values). If formulas exist and must remain functional after modification, exclusively use `openpyxl` for both reading and writing. Do not introduce `pandas` into the pipeline for such files unless you reconstruct all formulas afterward.

### Output Validation Strategy
After generating output files for spreadsheet tasks, verify not only that data values match expectations but also that:
- Row and column counts match expected delta
- Cell formatting remains intact (run visual or programmatic style checks)
- Formulas evaluate correctly post-operation
- Original sheet structure (merged cells, sheet order) is preserved
